content.full_text:
October 20, 1977

John Salemi  
Nossaman, Krueger & Marsh  
445 South Figueroa Street  
Los Angeles, CA 90071

Dear Mr. Salemi:

This letter is in response to our telephone conversation on September 22, 1977, regarding how Laughlin Waters, a member of your firm who was a registered lobbyist, should have reported salaries, fees and retainers received for lobbying in June and July of 1976.

You have advised us that Mr. Waters was a member of the law firm during the months in question and as such, did not receive a salary, but instead shared in the profits of the partnership. Periodically, throughout the year, Mr. Waters drew funds against his earnings in lieu of a salary.

In our phone conversation you stated that based on advice provided by a member of the Commission staff, Mr. Waters reported on his June and July 1976 lobbyist reports the percentage of time he devoted to lobbying, the name of the client for whom the lobbying work was performed and indicated that his share of the partnership earnings for the current year attributable to lobbying would exceed a specified minimum amount. Subsequent to the close of the partnership's fiscal year, Mr. Waters filed a supplement to those reports indicating the exact amount of his actual earnings that should be attributed to lobbying for each month he performed lobbying services. This was calculated by multiplying the average monthly income by the percentage of time spent on lobbying during the month in question.

Upon further reflection, we believe the manner of reporting utilized in your supplemental statement is the better approach. If this situation arises in the future, therefore, we would recommend that Mr. Waters report a monthly income figure by projecting his partnership earnings for the year, dividing that sum by 12, and then multiplying the quotient by the percentage of time spent that month on lobbying. The average monthly earnings of the individual from the prior year could be used as a measure of the projected current year's earnings provided no extraordinary fluctuations are anticipated. A brief explanation should be attached to the report to describe the method of reporting.

Although Mr. Water's reporting in June and July of 1976 varied slightly from this approach, we consider it to be in substantial compliance with the Act since it represents a good faith effort, based on advice from the Commission staff, to make full and adequate disclosure.

If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative

AH:vh
